known_memory_modes: Gear 2 commonly used for DDR5 overclocking
imc_limit_notes: Non-K or lower-bin CPUs may have reduced memory voltage/control flexibility.
quirks: High DDR5 speeds are usually limited by the CPU memory controller and motherboard topology before raw DIMM capability.
Two-slot and one-DIMM-per-channel boards usually train higher than four-DIMM layouts.
Final tWR and tWTR values may be produced by controls named tWRPRE, tWTP, tWRRD, or vendor equivalents.
timing_alias_behavior: BIOS vendors may split tRCD into read/write and expose tWRPRE/tWRRD controls.
recommended_testing_notes: This app does not validate stability; use independent memory stress tests.

sources.notes: Certified XMP examples and Intel DDR5 OC context.

supported_notes: DDR5 tuning depends heavily on CPU IMC
imc_limit_notes: High-frequency scaling improves on many samples but remains silicon lottery.
quirks: Very high MT/s usually favors 1DPC and two-slot boards.
Four-DIMM or heavy dual-rank setups usually reduce frequency headroom.
CPU VDDQ, TX VDDQ, and VDD2/MC voltage names vary by BIOS vendor.
timing_alias_behavior: tWRRD/tWTR and tWRPRE/tWR relationships can be BIOS-dependent.
recommended_testing_notes: Validate memory controller and DIMM stability separately.

sources.notes: Certified XMP examples and Intel DDR5 OC context.

supported_notes: Newer platform; keep board BIOS and memory QVL context in mind.
known_memory_modes: High-frequency DDR5 support is board and IMC dependent
imc_limit_notes: Public tuning data is still evolving.
quirks: Treat high-frequency claims as board, BIOS, and CPU-sample specific.
Detailed daily voltage guidance is still less mature than Alder/Raptor Lake community data.
timing_alias_behavior: Vendor BIOS naming differences remain important.
recommended_testing_notes: Use conservative ranges until more platform-specific data is added.

sources.notes: Certified XMP examples and Intel DDR5 OC context.

supported_notes: AM5 is DDR5-only; EXPO profiles are overclocking profiles.
common_frequency_zones: higher speeds often use divider tradeoffs
imc_limit_notes: DDR5-6000 is a widely cited Zen 4 sweet spot; above that
quirks: MT/s is double data rate; real memory clock is MT/s divided by two.
DDR5-6000 to 6400 is the common low-latency zone when UCLK remains 1:1.
Higher MT/s can move UCLK to 1:2, trading latency for bandwidth.
Prefer direct tWR, tWTRS, and tWTRL fields when exposed; do not map Intel tertiary names one-to-one.
Keep SoC voltage conservative; high SoC is CPU-side pressure, not DIMM voltage.
timing_alias_behavior: ZenTimings names may differ from motherboard BIOS labels.
recommended_testing_notes: Use external memory stress testing; this app only explains and compares settings.

sources.notes: AMD positions EXPO as DDR5 memory overclocking profiles for AM5.

sources.source_name: TechPowerUp report of AMD DDR5-6000 sweet spot AMA
sources.notes: Reports Robert Hallock comments on DDR5-6000 and clocks.

sources.source_name: AMD Ryzen Master Overclocking User Guide
sources.notes: AMD voltage/frequency overclocking caution and control names.

supported_notes: DDR5-only AM5 platform with similar clock-domain concepts to Zen 4.
quirks: UCLK/MCLK divider behavior remains central to latency.
High-bandwidth 8000+ MT/s profiles are workload dependent, not universally lower latency.
timing_alias_behavior: BIOS and ZenTimings labels may differ.
